EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across Southern Canada during the early morning hours of the 5th. The low pressure combined with high pressure over Eastern Idaho created a tight pressure gradient across Western Montana. This strong surface pressure gradient resulted in strong southwest winds across the valleys of South Central Montana.
Strong low pressure that moved across Southern Canada caused a brief period of very strong winds across the Livingston and Nye areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 50 mph were reported at the Livingston Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved across South Central Montana on the 13th. The storm system brought in a period of heavy snow, blowing snow and bitterly cold temperatures. The heaviest snow fell at the time of the strongest winds behind the frontal passage on the 13th, resulting in the greatest impacts. Winds of 25 to 35 mph, with gusts exceeding 50 mph were common, resulting in frequent visibilities at or below a quarter of a mile in many areas. Snowfall amounts were generaly in the one to four inch range with locally higher amounts. One fatality was reported along with two injuries in a 2 vehicle accident on Highway 212, about halfway between Laurel and Silesia. In addition, bitterly cold temperatures moved into the area for the remainder of the weekend. On the 15th, record low temperatures were reported at some locations as temperatures dropped to around 20 degrees below zero. The following locations all reported snow with wind gusts to 40 mph along with visibilities of less than 1/4 mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow with wind gusts of 40 mph and visibilities < 1/4 mile were observed in Colstrip and Forsyth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cascade fire started at approximately 215pm on July 26th. The cause of the fire is unknown. The fire was located in the West Fork of Rock Creek approximately 12 miles west of Red Lodge. The fire was difficult to fight as it was in very rugged terrain in addition to a lot of fuel from a blowndown wind event in the area in November of 2007. An Evacuation Immediate EAS activation was requested and disseminated at 212pm on July 27th for notification of a mandatory evacuation of residents near the fire. Five residential structures believed to be summer homes were destroyed. The wildfire was 100% contained by 8am, August 10th. Total acreage burned was 10,173 acres.

The Dunn Mountain Wildfire was sparked by lightning on Friday afternoon around 3pm, August 22nd. The wildfire was located 30 miles northeast of Billings. Firefighters made good progress on the blaze the first few days and were close to containment before a passing cold front on Monday evening, August 25th, produced significant winds and exponential fire growth. By Saturday night, August 30th, the fire had grown to 102,383 acres. Fire officials estimated that 12 structures were saved as a result of suppression efforts. However, five structures, including one trailer and four outbuildings were destroyed during extreme fire behavior between Friday and Saturday nights (August 29th and 30th). The fire fuels consisted of tall grass, dead and down timber with pockets of brush. Firefighters completed preliminary lines around the most actively growing section of the fire, east of Fattig Creek, Sunday, September 1st. Up to an inch of rain fell Sunday evening and Monday (September 1st and 2nd). As a result, fire officials felt secure enough about containment lines to declare the fire 100% contained. Mop-up and rehabilitation work continued for weeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of showers and thunderstorms moved across the northern areas of the city of Billings producing wind damage across portions of Billings Heights. The damage was confirmed to be the result of microburst winds with speeds up to 90 mph. A microburst is a sudden downburst of winds associated with thunderstorms. Microbursts can produce damage similar to weak tornadoes. ||This microburst impacted a four to five block area in the Heights that was centered around Solita Drive and Adobe Drive. Numerous houses had shingles blown off along with siding damage. One house experienced broken windows as a result of flying debris. Widespread damage to fencing in the area was also noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong arctic cold front moved into Northeast Montana in the early morning hours of Saturday December 13, 2008. The cold front brought snow, very strong winds, and extreme cold temperatures to the region. Blizzard conditions occurred throughout the day Saturday into Sunday with extreme wind chills experienced towards the end of the blizzard event.  Winds speed were generally stronger north of the Missouri River, but overall winds were 25 to 40 mph with continuous wind gusts up to 50 mph. Visibilities were below a quarter mile for the majority of the event. Snow totals ranged from 4 and 12 inches with drifts in some areas up to 3 feet.  Hinsdale had the most snow with 12 inches, and the highest wind gust was 54 mph at the Manning Corral site in southern Phillips County.  Roads were closed throughout the area during the height of the blizzard on the afternoon of the 13th through the morning of the 15th, including Montana Highway 191 north and south of Malta, US Highway 2 around Malta, Montana Highway 24 north of Glasgow and Montana Highway 5 from Opheim to the Daniels/Sheridan County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of four to seven inches were reported in the county.  Winds were in the 20 to 30 mph range with frequent gusts over 40 mph, reducing visibility to less than a quarter mile through much of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest air of the winter season to date arrived with the passage of an arctic front. In addition, a moist Pacific storm system moved across the area. The combination of these two features resulted in accumulating snow to much of the Billings Forecast Area. In addition, strong gusty winds resulted in blowing snow and bitter wind chills across the area. The following snow amounts were received: 6 inches 8SW Big Timber, 22SW Lodge Grass and in Hardin, 10 inches in Harlowton, 2N Harlowton and 10N Absarokee, 3 inches with blowing snow and bad travel conditions in Livingston, 7 inches in Laurel, 8 inches in Fort Smith and 1E Pray, 5 inches in Judith Gap with blowing snow, reduced visibilities and gusts to 56 mph, 12 inches 12S Livingston, 18 inches 14S Livingston, and 12 inches 22SW Lodge Grass.

An extremely strong arctic front moved into Montana in the early morning hours of Saturday December 13, 2008. Blizzard conditions occurred with the frontal passage while the coldest air of the season moved in behind the snowfall.  As the snow tapered off the wind chill temperatures started dropping to below 40 below zero and continued from Sunday December 14th through Monday December 15th.  Wind chills on Monday morning ranged from 40 to 55 below zero. The coldest locations was the Lindsay Divide Montana Department of Transportation site at 55 below zero. Area schools either canceled classes on Monday morning, or did not run school buses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest recorded wind chill was 45 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 3 and 4, a winter weather system moved across the mountains of Western Montana and produced heavy snowfall accumulations and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather stations reported between 10 and 12 inches of new snow at Lookout Pass, Noxon, and Evaro. In addition, wind gusts along the Interstate 90 Corridor near Lookout Pass gusted between 23 and 37 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation reported between 8 and 12 inches of new snow along the Montana Highway 83 Corridor between Clearwater Junction and Condon. Poor visibility from blowing and drifting snow was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic front pushed through western Montana, bringing a quick burst of snow, strong winds, and reduced visibility due to blowing and drifting snow. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 6 to 12 inches in most valleys, with up to 15 inches over higher terrain. Blizzard conditions were reported in the West Glacier Region and the northern Flathead Valley as wind gusts up to 45 mph were reported. The Montana|DOT reported severe driving conditions on area roads near the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was difficult to measure due to the blowing and drifting nature, but estimates were anywhere from 3 to 7 inches.  The main impact came from strong east to northeast winds measured at sustained 20 mph gusting up to 40 mph.  Visibility down to near zero was reported in many locations due to the blowing snow.  Many area roads were closed, including Highway 35 between Polson and the junction with S-206 where many trees were blown down onto the road from strong winds.  One person was killed on this road when a tree blew onto his vehicle. Two other vehicles were also damaged from falling trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved across South Central Montana on the 13th. The storm system brought in a period of heavy snow, blowing snow and bitterly cold temperatures. The heaviest snow fell at the time of the strongest winds behind the frontal passage on the 13th, resulting in the greatest impacts. Winds of 25 to 35 mph, with gusts exceeding 50 mph were common, resulting in frequent visibilities at or below a quarter of a mile in many areas. Snowfall amounts were generaly in the one to four inch range with locally higher amounts. One fatality was reported along with two injuries in a 2 vehicle accident on Highway 212, about halfway between Laurel and Silesia. In addition, bitterly cold temperatures moved into the area for the remainder of the weekend. On the 15th, record low temperatures were reported at some locations as temperatures dropped to around 20 degrees below zero. The following locations all reported snow with wind gusts to 40 mph along with visibilities of less than 1/4 mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow with wind gusts of 40 mph and visibilities < 1/4 mile were observed in Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another Pacific System brough ample moisture over portions of Western Montana to produce heavy snow accumulations. Snowfall accumulations ranged from widespread 6 to 10 inches in the valleys, up to 16 inches at Lost Trail Pass. Wind gusts up to 50 mph were reported in West Central and Southwest Montana causing reduced visibility and poor driving conditions from blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters and COOP observer reported new snow amounts between 7 and 12 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer and spotters reported 6 to 10 inches of new snow accumulations.  In addition, winds gusted from the west up to 50 mph.  The combination of snow and winds caused power outages across the Bitterroot Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tumble Creek Wildfire started approximately 420pm on the afternoon of August 1st. It's location was in the West Fork of the Stillwater River drainage approximately 8 miles west of the Woodbine Campground, and 1 mile south of the Absaroka/Beartooth Wilderness boundary. The wildfire was lightning caused and burned around 380 acres and was contained on August 8th. The fuels involved were timber (litter and understory).

The Knowlton Complex was lightning caused on the evening of August 21st. The fire was approximately 45 miles east and south of Miles City. Acreage burned was estimated at 6,907 acres. The fire was 100% contained approximately 7pm on August 24th. Fuels involved with the wildfire were broken, timbered terrain; grasslands and croplands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic front pushed through western Montana, bringing a quick burst of snow, strong winds, and reduced visibility due to blowing and drifting snow. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 6 to 12 inches in most valleys, with up to 15 inches over higher terrain. Blizzard conditions were reported in the West Glacier Region and the northern Flathead Valley as wind gusts up to 45 mph were reported. The Montana|DOT reported severe driving conditions on area roads near the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations between 7 and 10 inches was reported by local storm spotters.  Winds were sustained up to 20 mph with gusts up to 30 mph.  The DOT reported low visibility along Highway 83 between Clearwater Junction and Condon due to blowing and drifting snow.

Montana DOT reported severe driving conditions on Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved across South Central Montana on the 13th. The storm system brought in a period of heavy snow, blowing snow and bitterly cold temperatures. The heaviest snow fell at the time of the strongest winds behind the frontal passage on the 13th, resulting in the greatest impacts. Winds of 25 to 35 mph, with gusts exceeding 50 mph were common, resulting in frequent visibilities at or below a quarter of a mile in many areas. Snowfall amounts were generaly in the one to four inch range with locally higher amounts. One fatality was reported along with two injuries in a 2 vehicle accident on Highway 212, about halfway between Laurel and Silesia. In addition, bitterly cold temperatures moved into the area for the remainder of the weekend. On the 15th, record low temperatures were reported at some locations as temperatures dropped to around 20 degrees below zero. The following locations all reported snow with wind gusts to 40 mph along with visibilities of less than 1/4 mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sucker Creek and Dome Creek Snotels reported eight to ten inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely strong arctic front moved into Montana in the early morning hours of Saturday December 13, 2008. Blizzard conditions occurred with the frontal passage while the coldest air of the season moved in behind the snowfall.  As the snow tapered off the wind chill temperatures started dropping to below 40 below zero and continued from Sunday December 14th through Monday December 15th.  Wind chills on Monday morning ranged from 40 to 55 below zero. The coldest locations was the Lindsay Divide Montana Department of Transportation site at 55 below zero. Area schools either canceled classes on Monday morning, or did not run school buses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest wind chill was 48 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved into the region on the afternoon of December 29, 2008. The heaviest snow fell in the counties along and north of the Missouri River. Amounts ranged from six to ten inches of snow.  There were areas of blowing and drifting snow during the event, but visibilities remained above a half mile through the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to nine inches of snowfall was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong arctic cold front moved into Northeast Montana in the early morning hours of Saturday December 13, 2008. The cold front brought snow, very strong winds, and extreme cold temperatures to the region. Blizzard conditions occurred throughout the day Saturday into Sunday with extreme wind chills experienced towards the end of the blizzard event.  Winds speed were generally stronger north of the Missouri River, but overall winds were 25 to 40 mph with continuous wind gusts up to 50 mph. Visibilities were below a quarter mile for the majority of the event. Snow totals ranged from 4 and 12 inches with drifts in some areas up to 3 feet.  Hinsdale had the most snow with 12 inches, and the highest wind gust was 54 mph at the Manning Corral site in southern Phillips County.  Roads were closed throughout the area during the height of the blizzard on the afternoon of the 13th through the morning of the 15th, including Montana Highway 191 north and south of Malta, US Highway 2 around Malta, Montana Highway 24 north of Glasgow and Montana Highway 5 from Opheim to the Daniels/Sheridan County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals were from five to eight inches of snow.  Drifts were reported to be up to 5 feet in some areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of new snow with visibilities of a quarter mile to a half mile through the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 10 and 11, a strong Pacific storm system moved southeast across Western Montana and produced heavy snowfall amounts in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lookout Pass Ski Resort reported 8 inches of new snow. The Montana Department of Highways website reported 7.8 inches of new snowfall accumulations at Lookout Pass. A weather spotter in Heron reported 16 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 15 and 16, a vigorous winter storm moved across the mountains of Western Montana producing heavy snowfall and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotters at Essex, West Glacier, and 10 miles north of Columbia Falls reported 6 to 8 inches of new snow over a 12 hour period. In addition, weather observation stations at Fielding, Essex, and Swan Lake reported gusty winds between 28 and 33 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 15 and 16, a vigorous winter storm moved across the mountains of Western Montana producing heavy snowfall and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass was closed to all but emergency travel during this event from heavy snow and poor visibility. Wind gusts to 66 mph were recorded at the Little Rock Creek weather observation site which blew down 10 large trees. Lost Trail Pass received 6 inches of new snowfall accumulations over a 12 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the area ahead of a major winter storm. Light freezing rain intensified to produce significant freezing rain for this region.  Accumulations ranged from a tenth of an inch to a quarter of an inch of rain.  Roads north, south, east and west of Wolf Point were listed on the Montana Department of Transportation road reports as being covered in black ice.  There were numerous reports of cars sliding off of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 19 and 20, an Arctic front and and Pacific storm system collided over Western Montana. Heavy snowfall, whiteout conditions, and dangerous wind chills were observed at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe weather spotters in Ovando reported 6.5 inches of new snow and whiteout conditions during this event. Six inches of new snow was reported in Anaconda. Frequent wind gusts to 37 mph were observed along the Interstate 90 corridor near Deer Lodge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotters in Kalispell reported between 6 and 8 inches of new snow. 10 inches of new snow was reported 10 miles north of Bigfork. Winds across the Flathead valley frequently gusted between 33 and 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely strong arctic front moved into Montana in the early morning hours of Saturday December 13, 2008. Blizzard conditions occurred with the frontal passage while the coldest air of the season moved in behind the snowfall.  As the snow tapered off the wind chill temperatures started dropping to below 40 below zero and continued from Sunday December 14th through Monday December 15th.  Wind chills on Monday morning ranged from 40 to 55 below zero. The coldest locations was the Lindsay Divide Montana Department of Transportation site at 55 below zero. Area schools either canceled classes on Monday morning, or did not run school buses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest wind chill was 48 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest air of the winter season to date arrived with the passage of an arctic front. In addition, a moist Pacific storm system moved across the area. The combination of these two features resulted in accumulating snow to much of the Billings Forecast Area. In addition, strong gusty winds resulted in blowing snow and bitter wind chills across the area. The following snow amounts were received: 6 inches 8SW Big Timber, 22SW Lodge Grass and in Hardin, 10 inches in Harlowton, 2N Harlowton and 10N Absarokee, 3 inches with blowing snow and bad travel conditions in Livingston, 7 inches in Laurel, 8 inches in Fort Smith and 1E Pray, 5 inches in Judith Gap with blowing snow, reduced visibilities and gusts to 56 mph, 12 inches 12S Livingston, 18 inches 14S Livingston, and 12 inches 22SW Lodge Grass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow was reported in Wilsall, although strong wind combined with the snow to produce considerable blowing and drifting snow and bitter cold wind chills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Janauary 30 and 31, another potent winter storm moved across Western Montana and produced heavy snowfall, gusty winds, and signficant blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation declared emergency travel only along the US Highway 93 corridor south of Lolo due to blowing and drifting snow. Wind gusts between 23 mph and 36 mph were observed across the Missoula and Bitterroot valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Janauary 30 and 31, another potent winter storm moved across Western Montana and produced heavy snowfall, gusty winds, and signficant blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation declared emergency travel only over Lookout Pass due to poor visibility in snow and blowing snow. The automated weather station at Lookout Pass reported 13 inches, Saint Regis 11 inches, and Mullan Pass 8 inches of new snowfall respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Janauary 30 and 31, another potent winter storm moved across Western Montana and produced heavy snowfall, gusty winds, and signficant blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotters and automated weather stations reported between 6 and 13 inches of new snow between Lolo Hot Springs and Lolo Pass. Twelve Mile Creek Snotel reported 6.5 inches of new snow. Tepee Point and Sula automated weather stations recorded wind gusts between 32 mph and 39 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest air of the winter season to date arrived with the passage of an arctic front. In addition, a moist Pacific storm system moved across the area. The combination of these two features resulted in accumulating snow to much of the Billings Forecast Area. In addition, strong gusty winds resulted in blowing snow and bitter wind chills across the area. The following snow amounts were received: 6 inches 8SW Big Timber, 22SW Lodge Grass and in Hardin, 10 inches in Harlowton, 2N Harlowton and 10N Absarokee, 3 inches with blowing snow and bad travel conditions in Livingston, 7 inches in Laurel, 8 inches in Fort Smith and 1E Pray, 5 inches in Judith Gap with blowing snow, reduced visibilities and gusts to 56 mph, 12 inches 12S Livingston, 18 inches 14S Livingston, and 12 inches 22SW Lodge Grass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow was reported in Red Lodge, although strong winds combined with the snow to produce considerable blowing and drifting snow and bitter cold wind chills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely strong arctic front moved into Montana in the early morning hours of Saturday December 13, 2008. Blizzard conditions occurred with the frontal passage while the coldest air of the season moved in behind the snowfall.  As the snow tapered off the wind chill temperatures started dropping to below 40 below zero and continued from Sunday December 14th through Monday December 15th.  Wind chills on Monday morning ranged from 40 to 55 below zero. The coldest locations was the Lindsay Divide Montana Department of Transportation site at 55 below zero. Area schools either canceled classes on Monday morning, or did not run school buses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest recorded wind chill was 51 degrees below zero.

Very strong winds combined with low humidities fueled multiple wildfires across northeast Montana this afternoon.  Two of these fires grew out of control for several hours and destroyed a combined 5000 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began 12 miles north of Wolf Point and grew to approximately 1500 acres before terminating 14 miles east-northeast of Wolf Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and cold front moved through Southwest and West Central Montana on April 19 through April 20. This storm produced heavy snow accumulations and gusty winds across these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 90 was closed down to emergency travel only from Warm Springs, MT to just south of Opportunity, MT. This was due to blowing and drifting snow and poor visibility.

This combined with snow already on the ground, produced blizzard conditions. Whiteout conditions with visibilities of 1/8 to 1/4 mile throughout the afternoon and evening resulted in some children being stranded at school.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions with visibilities of 1/8 to 1/4 mile were observed throughout the day. Children were stranded at school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized wind damage occurred to two trailers on the sales lot of Rangitsch Brothers Home Center in Missoula, MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gust to 75 mph from a rain shower over the Rangitsch Brothers Home Center flipped one trailer over in the sales lot. Two additional trailers were moved. Major damage was observed to the trailer that was flipped over. Minor damage was observed on one other trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late winter storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to a portion of North Central Montana. Snow amounts greater than 6 inches were common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy snow, gusty winds and extreme cold to a large portion of North Central Montana. Snowfall amounts in excess of 6 inches, wind gusts over 40 mph and extremely cold temperatures were reported. The temperature fell to 8 below zero in Great Falls which is the second coldest April reading of all time for the city. The extreme cold was responsible for the deaths of lambs and livestock as well as crippling many trees and flowering plants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at Norris 15NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and cold front moved through Southwest and West Central Montana on April 19 through April 20. This storm produced heavy snow accumulations and gusty winds across these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass Snotel Observation reported 8.2 inches of storm total snow. The snow plow crew at Lost Trail Pass reported 8.5 inches of storm total snow. The Combination Snotel observation reported 9.5 inches of storm total snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds combined with low humidities fueled multiple wildfires across northeast Montana this afternoon.  Two of these fires grew out of control for several hours and destroyed a combined 5000 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started 14 miles north-northwest of Vida and grew to 3500 acres before being extinguished approximately 15 miles northeast of Vida.  Two separate property owners suffered minor burns while attempting to control the fire and a total of ten outbuildings were lost.  Fencing and power poles were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front produced numerous high-based thunderstorms. The storms produced numerous outflows and strong to severe wind gusts, and one gustnado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shallow column of swirling dirt and dust, or gustnado, was observed by several witnesses along an outflow boundaries to the southeast of Carter along the Highwood bench. Was on the ground for about 7 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and cold front moved through Southwest and West Central Montana on April 19 through April 20. This storm produced heavy snow accumulations and gusty winds across these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana Highway 12 from Garrison Junction, MT to Avon, MT was closed to emergency travel only due to blowing, drifting snow and poor visibility. Sustained winds of 30 mph gusting up to 37 mph was reported at Garrison Junction during this time. Interstate 90 was closed down for emergency travel only from Phosphate, MT to Warm Springs, MT including the town of Deer Lodge, MT due to blowing and drifting snow with poor visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late spring storm system tracked slowly across northern Wyoming and South Dakota bringing much needed precipitation to portions of northeast Montana.  The precipitation began early in the morning as rain before changing to heavy wet snow in the afternoon across Valley, Garfield, McCone and Prairie counties.  Most locations in these counties measured snow ranging between two and four inches by late evening; although heavier amounts of eight to nine inches were recorded in areas with higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall ranged between four and six inches 15 miles east of Crow Rock.  The snow and cold temperatures resulted in the death of several newborn calves in the higher elevations of the Big Sheep Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level trough over the northern Rockies supported multiple rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across portions of central and northeast Montana.  The excess water caused the Musselshell River near Mosby to briefly rise one foot above flood stage on the morning of the 25th before returning below flood stage that afternoon.  Also, flooding in Zortman resulted in a few area dirt roads being washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed on a few area dirt roads resulting in portions of the road surfaces washing out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front combined with gusty northwest winds brought bitter wind chills to portions of northeast Montana on the evening of February 8th.  They died down briefly and picked up again by late in the morning through early evening on February 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two firefighters were struck by lightning while conducting mop up of a prescribed burn west of Whitefish, MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two firefighters, a 25 year old woman and a 29 year old man, members of the Flahtead National Forest were struck by lightning while finishing up a mop up of a prescribed burn west of Whitefish, MT and heading back to their vehicles. The lightning directly hit two nearby trees and injuried the firefighters. The two firefighters were taken to the hospital and listed as in stable condition.

An upper level trough and associated surface cold front moved across Western Montana beginning on the evening of June 11 and continuing through the day on June 12. This brought heavy snow accumulations throughout the area. Power outages occurred in northwest Montana as heavy snow caused tree damage from the northern Flathead Valley southward to Evaro Hill and Greenough Park near Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flathead County dispatch reported 7 inches of new snowfall in Marion, MT. Tree damage and power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and associated surface cold front moved across Western Montana beginning on the evening of June 11 and continuing through the day on June 12. This brought heavy snow accumulations throughout the area. Power outages occurred in northwest Montana as heavy snow caused tree damage from the northern Flathead Valley southward to Evaro Hill and Greenough Park near Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various heavy snow reports were taken across the West Glacier Region from trained spotters, the general public, the Montana Department of Transportation and automated observing stations. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4.5 inches in Hungry Horse to 10 inches at Essex and Swan Lake at the lower elevations. In the mountains, snowfall ranged from 12 inches at Moss Peak, 24 inches above the Loop to 40 inches at Badger Pass. The heavy snow brought down trees on the Sun Road and other routes in Glacier National Park.

The following snow reports were received: 6 inches 10W Livingston, 10S Livingston and in Livingston, 8 inches 12S Livingston, and 12 inches at the Shower Falls Snotel.

Pacific moisture combined with a storm system that dropped south from Canada brought heavy snow to the Beartooth and Absaroka Mountains and snow and blowing snow to portions of central Montana. The following snow totals were reported: 7 inches 19NW Silver Gate(Monument Peak Snotel), 7 inches 22SW Limestone(Box Canyon Snotel), 8 inches 3NNW Cooke City(Fisher Creek Snotel) and 8 inches 10WSW Limestone(Placer Basin Snotel). In the lower elevations, the snow and blowing snow resulting in numerous road and school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency travel was advised for the interstate.

In the lower elevations, the snow and blowing snow resulting in numerous road and school closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong spring storm system brought significant precipitation to southeast Montana and portions of south central Montana during the first two days of the month. The heaviest precipitation occurred over the higher terrain of southeast Montana. Enough cold air was generated by the storm system that heavy snow developed. In addition, deep surface low pressure that was situated over western South Dakota resulted in strong northwest surface winds. These strong winds that combined with the snow resulted in blizzard conditions and whiteout conditions at times. Total losses for this storm are not known except for Carter County where upwards of $3 million of losses were reported. ||The following is a comprehensive list of snow reports: 12 inches 24SE Ekalaka, Ridgeway, 9 ESE Webster, and in Alzada, 7 inches (2.85 of liquid) in Belltower, 6 inches 12S Plevna, and 10SE Roundup, 6 inches in Volborg with strong winds and wet snow on power poles and power lines causing three power poles to break, 8 inches in Melstone and 1S Red Lodge, 26 inches in Ekalaka (100 power poles down with 90% of the county without power. Highway 7 was closed.), 11 inches 3 ENE Mill Iron and 3E Lame Deer, 24 inches 8S Ekalaka, 9 inches in Rock Springs,  10 inches 1N Volborg(lost calf to snow), Angela, Lame Deer, 15W Biddle(lost calf to snow), and in Sonnette, and 6 inches 10N Decker.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: East Livingston DOT measured 51 mph sustained winds. At 621 pm, wind gusts were estimated over 60 mph. Interstate 90 was closed from Exit 330 to 338 with reports of multiple accidents with at least four semi trailers blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest air of the winter season to date arrived with the passage of an arctic front. In addition, a moist Pacific storm system moved across the area. The combination of these two features resulted in accumulating snow to much of the Billings Forecast Area. In addition, strong gusty winds resulted in blowing snow and bitter wind chills across the area. The following snow amounts were received: 6 inches 8SW Big Timber, 22SW Lodge Grass and in Hardin, 10 inches in Harlowton, 2N Harlowton and 10N Absarokee, 3 inches with blowing snow and bad travel conditions in Livingston, 7 inches in Laurel, 8 inches in Fort Smith and 1E Pray, 5 inches in Judith Gap with blowing snow, reduced visibilities and gusts to 56 mph, 12 inches 12S Livingston, 18 inches 14S Livingston, and 12 inches 22SW Lodge Grass.

A very strong storm system over Colorado resulted in high winds across portions of the far Eastern Plains during a 48 hour period from May 21st through the morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph were reported across southeast Powder River County. Sustained winds of 50 mph were reported in Biddle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure developed over Central Montana. This trough along with high pressure over Southeast Idaho and Western Wyoming brought strong winds to the Upper Yellowstone River Valley. Numerous reports of sustained winds in excess of 50 mph were noted at the Livingston ASOS and the East Livingston DOT site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of sustained winds in excess of 50 mph were noted at the Livingston ASOS and the East Livingston DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving storm system brought heavy snow to the mountains in and around Cooke City. The following snow amounts were received: 8 inches 2N Cooke City(White Mill Snotel), and 9.5 inches 3NNW Cooke City(Fisher Creek Snotel).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms developed this day across portions of northeast Montana. The skies behind this first round of storms cleared by midday fueling the redevelopment of additional severe storms ahead of a surface trough.

An upper level trough and associated surface cold front moved across Western Montana beginning on the evening of June 11 and continuing through the day on June 12. This brought heavy snow accumulations throughout the area. Power outages occurred in northwest Montana as heavy snow caused tree damage from the northern Flathead Valley southward to Evaro Hill and Greenough Park near Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass Visitor Center reported 6 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong spring storm system brought significant precipitation to southeast Montana and portions of south central Montana during the first two days of the month. The heaviest precipitation occurred over the higher terrain of southeast Montana. Enough cold air was generated by the storm system that heavy snow developed. In addition, deep surface low pressure that was situated over western South Dakota resulted in strong northwest surface winds. These strong winds that combined with the snow resulted in blizzard conditions and whiteout conditions at times. Total losses for this storm are not known except for Carter County where upwards of $3 million of losses were reported. ||The following is a comprehensive list of snow reports: 12 inches 24SE Ekalaka, Ridgeway, 9 ESE Webster, and in Alzada, 7 inches (2.85 of liquid) in Belltower, 6 inches 12S Plevna, and 10SE Roundup, 6 inches in Volborg with strong winds and wet snow on power poles and power lines causing three power poles to break, 8 inches in Melstone and 1S Red Lodge, 26 inches in Ekalaka (100 power poles down with 90% of the county without power. Highway 7 was closed.), 11 inches 3 ENE Mill Iron and 3E Lame Deer, 24 inches 8S Ekalaka, 9 inches in Rock Springs,  10 inches 1N Volborg(lost calf to snow), Angela, Lame Deer, 15W Biddle(lost calf to snow), and in Sonnette, and 6 inches 10N Decker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous mid level shortwave trough entered northeast Montana this afternoon.  Extensive morning cloud cover prevented widespread severe thunderstorm development from this impulse, however marginal instability in a few areas supported some severe thunderstorms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 55 foot evergreen tree was uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass covering most of North central Montana allowed widespread severe weather to develop. Numerous severe thunderstorms were reported. Hail the size of golf balls and wind gusts in excess of 70 mph were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms, mainly producing large hail, moved across Southern Montana during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported flowing over Brandenberg and Tongue River Roads with 2.45 inches of rain being reported in two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level low pressure system moved across Western Montana on the evening and night of 07/01/2008. This low interacted with a moist and unstable atmosphere and resulted in severe thunderstorms across Northwest and West Central Montana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms, mainly producing large hail, moved across Southern Montana during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms producing large hail and severe wind gusts moved across Southeast Montana during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 18th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain and nickel size hail was reported 2 miles south of Otter.

Severe thunderstorms producing large hail and damaging winds moved across portions of Big Horn and Powder River Counties during the early evening hours of the 16th. Two fatalities, both drownings, occurred from a severe thunderstorm that moved across the Tongue River Reservoir. A man fell from a dock and a woman drowned when her small boat capsized as a result of very strong thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large Cottonwood trees were blown over and a few windows blown in on a house and at least one power pole was snapped due to strong thunderstorm winds 5 miles south of Wyola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong thunderstorm developed over Musselshell County and moved east into Custer County during the late evening hours of the 2nd and into the early morning hours of the 3rd. Some hail accompanied the storm but the main feature was very strong and damaging winds. Damaging outflow winds traveled as far as 100 miles south of the storm itself.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 87 mph was measured by ASOS at Miles City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A human-caused wildfire developed eight miles north of Dodson on August 25 and grew to approximately 5,500 acres.  The fire was started by an RV that had sparks of fire coming from the exhaust.  There were smaller fires along the highway from Bowdoin Wildlife Refuge into Malta, and then from Malta to Dodson.  The fire consumed mostly tall grass on private, state, and BLM lands.  Several ground-based fire crews along with aerial resources established control over the fire by the afternoon of the 26th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-five three inches of snow fell at Ennis 15NW (the Lower Twin SNOTEL site - elevation 7900 feet). Additionally, 23 inches of snow fell at Pony 6W (the Albro Lake SNOTEL site - elevation 8300 feet), 11 inches at West Yellowstone 22NW (the Carrot Basin SNOTEL site - elevation 9000 feet), and 8 inches at West Yellowstone 24NW (the Beaver Creek SNOTEL site - elevation 7850 feet.

An unseasonably strong area of low pressure in lower Saskatchewan delivered well below normal temperatures with strong northwest winds to northeast Montana.  Here is a list of peak wind gusts across the area: Glendive Airport: 51 mph, Lindsay: 50 mph, South Sawmill Creek: 53 mph, Jordan Airport: 51 mph, Fort Peck Dam: 54 mph, 10 miles south of Vida: 50 mph, 1 mile west of Zortman: 51 mph, 28 miles northwest of Terry: 54 mph, 28 miles north-northeast of Wolf Point: 55 mph, 7 miles east of Bainville: 50 mph, 33 miles southwest of Glasgow: 51 mph.  The Montana Governor's Cup Walleye Tournament on Fort Peck Lake had to be delayed by one day for the safety of the tournament participants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was taken from the mesonet site east of the Fort Peck Dam.  A boat near Fort Peck Lake was damaged from flying debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed this afternoon across central Montana ahead of a vigorous upper level trough.  A few of these storms eventually grew into a squall line and affected parts of northeast Montana producing damaging straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptic winds blew through the region during the evening hours started just after 9 pm, delaying firework displays through most northeast Montana communities.  Several small wildfires were caused by the public using fireworks during the windy conditions.  Glasgow and Hinsdale both reported fires that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low lifted out of the Great Basin on October 11th spreading a swath of heavy wet snow across northeast Montana.   The combination of heavy wet snow on several trees that had yet to lose their leaves created numerous power outages, small fires from the power lines arcing and broken tree limbs.  The slow movement of this low pressure system resulted in significant early season snowfall accumulations throughout most of the region with a record daily maximum snowfall for October having occurred in Glasgow. The majority of the reports were from 6 to 12 inches, with a few higher reports, including 16 inches in the Larb Hills southwest of Hinsdale, and 18 on the top of Big Sheep Mountain northwest of Terry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall total of 10 inches was estimated 18 miles northwest of Sidney.  Most reports were from 5 to 10 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm brought heavy wet and record snowfall to Southern Montana. As an upper low dropped south into southern Nevada Friday, October 10th, before making a turn to the northeast reaching Eastern Montana on October 12th, persistent overrunning and upslope flow brought a long duration snow event to the region. Snowfall began in many areas on the evening of Thursday, October 9th and continued through Sunday, October 12th. The most intense portion of the storm with the heaviest snowfall rates occurred Saturday night October 11th into Sunday morning October 12th as the main upper low ejected out across Montana. With the high water content of the snow, many large tree limbs and power lines were brought down by the weight of the snow. Temperatures were also well below normal for this time of year. An unseasonably strong upper level low lifted out of the Great Basin on October 11th spreading a swath of heavy wet snow across northeast Montana.   The combination of heavy wet snow on several trees that had yet to lose their leaves created numerous power outages, small fires from the power lines arcing and broken tree limbs.  The slow movement of this low pressure system resulted in significant early season snowfall accumulations throughout most of the region with a record daily maximum snowfall for October having occurred in Glasgow. The majority of the reports were from 6 to 12 inches, with a few higher reports, including 16 inches in the Larb Hills southwest of Hinsdale, and 18 on the top of Big Sheep Mountain northwest of Terry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snowfall was measured four miles northwest of Scobey.  Most reports in the county ranged from 5 to 10 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low lifted out of the Great Basin on October 11th spreading a swath of heavy wet snow across northeast Montana.   The combination of heavy wet snow on several trees that had yet to lose their leaves created numerous power outages, small fires from the power lines arcing and broken tree limbs.  The slow movement of this low pressure system resulted in significant early season snowfall accumulations throughout most of the region with a record daily maximum snowfall for October having occurred in Glasgow. The majority of the reports were from 6 to 12 inches, with a few higher reports, including 16 inches in the Larb Hills southwest of Hinsdale, and 18 on the top of Big Sheep Mountain northwest of Terry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 5 to 14 inches of snow were reported throughout the county.  Glendive had 5 inches of snow.   Additionally, two to three inches of slush were reported on Highway 254.  Poor road conditions resulted in eight cows being struck and killed by a truck traveling north on Highway 200 around mile marker 18. This happened Saturday evening on the 11th, also 2 more cows may need to be put down due to their injuries. The estimated value of each cow was $685.00 each.

An unseasonably strong upper level low lifted out of the Great Basin on October 11th spreading a swath of heavy wet snow across northeast Montana.   The combination of heavy wet snow on several trees that had yet to lose their leaves created numerous power outages, small fires from the power lines arcing and broken tree limbs.  The slow movement of this low pressure system resulted in significant early season snowfall accumulations throughout most of the region with a record daily maximum snowfall for October having occurred in Glasgow. The majority of the reports were from 6 to 12 inches, with a few higher reports, including 16 inches in the Larb Hills southwest of Hinsdale, and 18 on the top of Big Sheep Mountain northwest of Terry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall of 13.0 inches was measured at the NWS office in Glasgow.  The highest report was 16 inches 4 miles southwest of Hinsdale.  Most reports were in the 7 to 14 inch range across this portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low lifted out of the Great Basin on October 11th spreading a swath of heavy wet snow across northeast Montana.   The combination of heavy wet snow on several trees that had yet to lose their leaves created numerous power outages, small fires from the power lines arcing and broken tree limbs.  The slow movement of this low pressure system resulted in significant early season snowfall accumulations throughout most of the region with a record daily maximum snowfall for October having occurred in Glasgow. The majority of the reports were from 6 to 12 inches, with a few higher reports, including 16 inches in the Larb Hills southwest of Hinsdale, and 18 on the top of Big Sheep Mountain northwest of Terry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall was measured at 12 inches five miles south of Opheim. Most areas reported 6 to 12 inches of snowfall with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm brought heavy wet and record snowfall to Southern Montana. As an upper low dropped south into southern Nevada Friday, October 10th, before making a turn to the northeast reaching Eastern Montana on October 12th, persistent overrunning and upslope flow brought a long duration snow event to the region. Snowfall began in many areas on the evening of Thursday, October 9th and continued through Sunday, October 12th. The most intense portion of the storm with the heaviest snowfall rates occurred Saturday night October 11th into Sunday morning October 12th as the main upper low ejected out across Montana. With the high water content of the snow, many large tree limbs and power lines were brought down by the weight of the snow. Temperatures were also well below normal for this time of year. A strong jet stream ushered a very moist plume of moisture over western Montana.  Precipitation amounts of one to four inches fell over the Bitterroot Mountains, causing flash flooding in the lower terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ravalli County Road Department reported wash-outs on Middle Bear Creek Rd. (SW of Victor), Pleasant View Dr. (W of Victor), and Red Crow Rd. (SW of Victor).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front blasted across South Central and Southeast Montana on October 25th and 26th. Behind the front, very strong northwest winds brought sustained winds around 40 to 45 mph with gusts around 60 mph. The following wind reports were received: 46 mph sustained 11SSE Klein (Bull Mountain DOT), 42 mph sustained 2ESE Alzada(Alzada DOT), 59 mph gust 6ENE Lame Deer(Badger Peak RAWS), 65 mph gust 5ENE Chestnut(Bozeman Pass DOT), 41 mph sustained in Harlowton, 44 mph sustained 11SE Huntley(Arrow Creek RAWS), 56 mph sustained 5ENE Chestnut(Bozeman Pass DOT), 45 mph sustained 2WNW Reed Point(Reedpoint DOT), 48 mph sustained 11NNW Hardin(Aberdeen Hill DOT), 43 mph sustained in Baker and 44 mph sustained 2ESE Alzada(Alzada DOT).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of sustained winds of 40+ mph were reported 11 miles SE of Huntley at the Arrow Creek DOT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front brought strong winds and some isolated wind damage to Flathead County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flathead Electric Company reported a cottonwood tree blown down onto a mobile home on Evergreen Drive near Kalispell, causing damage to a side wall and the roof of the home.  Trees were also reported down onto power lines in Foyes Lake with 14 power outages in Flathead County.  Wind gusts of 45 to 50 mph were recorded from wind sensors in Flathead County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season cut off low brought heavy snow to southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across southwest Montana with 6 to 13 inches of snow reported from Philipsburg to near Butte. Montana Highway Patrol responded to 11 weather-related crashes in the area between 200 AM and 330 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft combined with daytime instability to generate scattered strong thunderstorms.  A few of these pulse-type storms became severe producing hail and one elderly man suffered minor injuries from a nearby lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail grew from pea to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass covering most of North central Montana allowed widespread severe weather to develop. Numerous severe thunderstorms were reported. Hail the size of golf balls and wind gusts in excess of 70 mph were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 60 mph at Belt 1W. Numerous tree branches were reported downed and power was out for an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving shortwave trough encountered a strong nocturnal low level jet resulting in the development of an intense squall line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was taken from the Sioux Pass DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of severe thunderstorms moved across the same locations as the previous evening (16th). Large hail was the main threat.

The Little Powder River experienced rapid rises just south of the Montana state line near Weston, Wyoming on May 24th and continued to run high through May 28th. The river rose rapidly in Biddle and was at bankfull, and at times, slightly above bankfull. This was a result of heavy rain upstream over Northeast Wyoming.

A strong shortwave trough located in southern Montana triggered a few severe thunderstorms in northeast Montana.  One of these storms quickly developed into a destructive bow echo near Miles City before continuing northeast into Prairie, Dawson and Wibaux counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This wind gust occurred well behind the leading edge of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms, mainly producing large hail, moved across Southern Montana during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicles damaged, large trees down, and State DOT garage doors were blown off due to hail and strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms producing large hail and damaging winds moved across portions of South Central and Southeast Montana during the late afternoon hours of the 22nd and continued into the early morning hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph were reported in Clyde Park. Some small tree limbs were reported down along with some scattered power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms producing large hail and a few funnel clouds moved across Southeast Montana during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 5th. The largest hail size reported was golf ball size.
A severe thunderstorm developed over Fergus County during the midafternoon hours of the 16th. Hail the size of golf balls was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped 1.75 inch diameter hail at Forest Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable and moist airmass allowd thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon hours. Hail the size of pennies and wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped .75 inch diameter hail at Lincoln 25NE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent closed low pressure system brought heavy rain to much of North Central Montana. Rainfall of 2 to 5 inches was common over the plains and valleys while rain amounts over 10 inches were reported in the mountains. The heavy rains caused many rivers and streams to overflow their banks for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dupuyer Creek over bankfull causing some lowland flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface dry line moved into northeast Montana and provided the focus for numerous severe thunderstorms.  Significant crop losses occurred with this event, but damage estimates and areal extent of the losses are not known.

An upper level shortwave trough exited lower Alberta and sparked several severe thunderstorms during the morning.  Additional storms redeveloped in the afternoon before quickly exiting into North Dakota.  Significant crop losses occurred with this event, but damage estimates and areal extent of the losses are not known.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground and stripped most of the leaves from the trees.  An estimated wind gust to 50 mph accompanied the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms producing large hail and damaging winds moved across portions of South Central and Southeast Montana during the late afternoon hours of the 22nd and continued into the early morning hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at Wild Bill and Blacktail Creeks along Powder River. In addition, two roads were washed out south of Biddle near the Montana/Wyoming border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms packing damaging winds moved across Central and Southeast Montana during the evening hours of the 26th and the early morning hours of the 27th. The strongest winds were estimated at 80 mph across portions of Treasure County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power was knocked out fourteen miles north of Pompeys Pillar as a result of a 69 mph thundestorm outflow gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level impulse generated numerous severe thunderstorms across northeast Montana.  A few of these storms produced hail as large as two inches in diameter with winds up to 80 mph.  Significant crop losses occurred with this event, but damage estimates and areal extent of the losses are not known.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained between 50 and 60 mph along with 0.75 hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed from an unstable airmass during the late afternoon/early evening hours of the 17th. Hail the size of golf balls was reported as well as wind gusts to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped 1.75 inch diameter hail at Roy 7NNE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over southwest Montana during the late afternoon hours. These storms were generally high-based and produced wind gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 67 mph at the Dillon ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon and early evening pulse severe thunderstorms developed over southwest Montana producing strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana Department of Transportation weather station on Highway 141 near Avon recorded peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across the southern zones during the entire evening of the 25th. Hail with thunderstorm wind gusts were the result.
Severe thunderstorms producing large hail and severe wind gusts moved across Southeast Montana during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts of 60 mph were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms developed this day across portions of northeast Montana.  The first severe storms developed ahead of a strong mid level disturbance early this morning before exiting into North Dakota by the early afternoon.  The skies behind this first round of storms cleared by midday fueling the redevelopment of additional severe storms ahead of a surface trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was relayed from a DES coordinator.
